name: Basic Block Versioning
summary: Maxime Chevalier-Boisvert's BBV: lazily generate per-block specialised variants based on observed types, without tracing or method-level versioning. Each basic block can have multiple type-specialised versions; selection happens per-edge based on the predecessor's specialisation. Powers Ruby's YJIT.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
**Basic Block Versioning** (Maxime Chevalier-Boisvert) lazily generates per-block specialised variants based on observed types — without tracing or method-level versioning. Each basic block can have multiple type-specialised versions; selection happens per-edge based on the predecessor block's specialisation context.

BBV's appeal: it captures most of tracing's path-specialised performance with simpler implementation. Where tracing JITs record long sequences of inlined operations and pay trace-explosion costs, BBV specialises one block at a time and compiles only the type combinations that actually occur at each edge.

sections.title: Lazy BBV
sections.content:
The original 2015 OOPSLA paper ("Simple and Effective Type Check Removal Through Lazy Basic Block Versioning") introduced the lazy variant: don't pre-compute all type combinations; specialise blocks on demand as new type contexts arrive.

The runtime starts with one unspecialised version of each basic block. When a block is reached with type context X, generate version-X if it doesn't exist, and link the predecessor's exit to the new version. As the program runs, the set of versions grows to cover the actually-occurring type combinations. Versions that prove rarely used can be discarded; commonly used ones become the steady-state JIT output.

sections.title: Ruby YJIT — production BBV
sections.content:
Ruby's YJIT (Chevalier-Boisvert et al., shipping in Ruby 3.1+) is the most prominent production deployment of BBV. The implementation is in Rust (since Ruby 3.2); typical workloads see 30–50% speedup over the standard interpreter, with predictable warm-up behaviour and low memory overhead compared to a method-level optimising JIT.

The lesson generalises: BBV is the right design point for languages where method-level specialisation is too coarse but full tracing is too elaborate. It hits a sweet spot of implementation simplicity, fast warm-up, and adequate steady-state performance for many dynamic-language workloads.
